card.title: Why '123456' Won't Save You
card.teaser: Your password isn't judged by how clever it feels — it's judged by how boring it is to a machine making a billion guesses a second.
card.coverImagePrompt: An ominous red padlock on a dark screen, streams of guessed passwords flowing like a waterfall behind it, the most common ones glowing brightest, cinematic cyber aesthetic

lessons.title: The dumbest attack in the world still works
lessons.body: Every year, security researchers analyze leaked password databases. And every year, the top choice is the same: '123456.' Not close to it — it. Around 1 in 200 users pick it. That means a hacker trying just one password against thousands of accounts will compromise several on the first try. Before anything fancy, before malware, before phishing — there's the simple fact that most humans are breathtakingly predictable.

visual.imagePrompt: A giant digital ticker counting down the world's most common passwords, '123456' frozen and glowing at the top

lessons.title: Entropy, measured in bits
lessons.body: Password strength has a precise unit: bits of entropy. Each bit doubles the number of possible passwords. A 4-digit PIN has about 13 bits — 10,000 possibilities. 'correcthorsebatterystaple,' a random combination of four common words, has about 44 bits — roughly 17 trillion possibilities. The math, not the memorability, is what protects you.

keyTerms.definition: A measure of unpredictability, in bits, where each bit doubles the search space
Trying every possible password until one works

visual.imagePrompt: A logarithmic graph comparing password strengths, with '123456' as a tiny bar and a four-word passphrase towering beside it

lessons.title: Hash functions and the guessing game
lessons.body: Servers don't store your password directly (at least, not if they're competent). They store a hash — a scrambled fingerprint that can't be reversed. When you log in, your typed password is hashed and compared. The catch: attackers who steal the database can hash billions of candidate passwords per second on a GPU and look for matches. A slow hash function like bcrypt dramatically raises the cost. A fast one like plain SHA-1 is catastrophic.

keyTerms.definition: A one-way fingerprint of a password
A deliberately slow hash designed to thwart brute-force attacks

visual.imagePrompt: A rack of GPUs glowing red, numbers streaming past at blurring speed as password hashes are tested

lessons.title: The RockYou leak
lessons.body: In 2009, a gaming site called RockYou was breached, exposing 32 million plaintext passwords. That single list became the starting point for virtually every password-cracking tool since. Nearly every 'creative' password you can think of — 'iloveyou,' 'letmein,' 'passw0rd' — has been in someone's dictionary for 15 years. If you can think of it, it's already been tried.
lessons.funFact: A modern GPU can test around 100 billion MD5 hashes per second — meaning every 8-character lowercase password on Earth can be broken in minutes.

visual.imagePrompt: A scrolling list of leaked passwords with the most common ones highlighted, red and black aesthetic

lessons.title: Length. Randomness. Second factor.
lessons.body: Long, random, and unique beats clever every time. Use a password manager to handle the entropy for you. Turn on two-factor authentication — even a broken password won't matter if an attacker also needs the code on your phone. The goal isn't to outsmart hackers. It's to make yourself expensive enough that they move on to the next target.

quiz.question: What does 'entropy' measure in a password?
options: How fast it can be typed
quiz.explanation: Entropy is a measurement of unpredictability — each additional bit doubles the number of possible passwords.

quiz.question: Why do security engineers prefer bcrypt over SHA-1 for passwords?
options: It's deliberately slow, which makes brute force far more expensive
It encrypts the password instead of hashing it
quiz.explanation: Slowness is a feature: it raises the cost per guess, defeating the GPU speed advantage attackers have.

quiz.question: What makes a passphrase like 'correcthorsebatterystaple' strong?
options: Its length and random word choice produce high entropy
quiz.explanation: Four randomly chosen common words yield tens of trillions of possibilities — enough to resist brute force.

sources.title: NIST Special Publication 800-63B: Digital Identity Guidelines
sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
